summary: Stancil argues that strict, uniform SQL formatting rules are less important than making a query’s logic legible to humans. Formatting should adapt to the structure and emphasis of the logic, even if that breaks conventional style guides or what a formatter can automate.
money_quotes: What’s the best way to format these parts of a query? The answer—an answer every analyst should love, if not many priests—is that it depends.
Queries—which, above all, transcribe vague business concepts, like revenue and win rate, into precise formulae—should also make their logic legible.
Formatting is our medium, and the medium is the message.
As query authors, consistency shouldn’t be our goal, no more than we should make it our goal to consistently use bar charts.
Rules, even the best documented and most hallowed ones, are sometimes best ignored.
key_insight: Effective SQL formatting is not about rigid rules but about highlighting the logic that matters, even if that means breaking conventions.
